questions.stem: Which statement explains why a reaction is exothermic?

parts.prompt: Which statement explains why a reaction is exothermic?

mcq_options.label: A
mcq_options.text: More thermal energy is absorbed by breaking bonds than is released by making bonds.

mcq_options.label: B
mcq_options.text: More thermal energy is absorbed by making bonds than is released by breaking bonds.

mcq_options.label: C
mcq_options.text: More thermal energy is released by breaking bonds than is absorbed by making bonds.

mcq_options.label: D
mcq_options.text: More thermal energy is released by making bonds than is absorbed by breaking bonds.

required_working_concepts.concept: Bond breaking absorbs energy (endothermic); bond making releases energy (exothermic)
Reaction is exothermic when energy released from making new bonds exceeds energy absorbed to break old bonds
hints: released by making > absorbed by breaking

common_mistakes.feedback: You picked A. That answer says energy is 'absorbed by breaking' (true) but 'released by making' (also true) — however exothermic needs the OPPOSITE inequality. Remember MEXOBENDO: for the reaction to give out heat, the making side (release) must be GREATER than the breaking side (absorb). The correct answer is D.

You picked B. That option says bond MAKING absorbs energy — this breaks the MEXOBENDO rule. Making bonds RELEASES energy; breaking bonds ABSORBS energy. So B has the endo/exo labels flipped. The correct answer is D.

You picked C. You correctly linked 'released' to an exothermic feel, but you attached it to BREAKING bonds. Breaking bonds ABSORBS energy (endothermic); making bonds RELEASES energy (exothermic). Use MEXOBENDO: Making = EXO, Breaking = ENDO. The correct answer is D.

parts.model_answer_html:
Answer: D. More thermal energy is released by making bonds than is absorbed by breaking bonds.

MEXOBENDO: Making bonds is EXOthermic (releases energy); Breaking bonds is ENDOthermic (absorbs energy).

Net energy change = energy released by making new bonds − energy absorbed by breaking old bonds.

For the reaction to be overall exothermic (gives out heat to the surroundings), the energy released by making must be greater than the energy absorbed by breaking — which is exactly what option D states.

  • A has the inequality the wrong way round — it describes an endothermic reaction.
  • B inverts MEXOBENDO (says making absorbs).
  • C inverts MEXOBENDO (says breaking releases).
chapter_refs: MEXOBENDO — bond breaking endothermic / bond making exothermic
C5.01 net energy change = energy released (making) − energy absorbed (breaking)
C5.01 exothermic condition: released-by-making > absorbed-by-breaking

questions.stem: Which label represents the activation energy for an endothermic reaction? (energy vs progress of reaction graph with labelled points A, B, C, D)

parts.prompt: Which label represents the activation energy for an endothermic reaction?

mcq_options.label: A
mcq_options.text: A

mcq_options.label: B
mcq_options.text: B

mcq_options.label: C
mcq_options.text: C

mcq_options.label: D
mcq_options.text: D

required_working_concepts.concept: endothermic profile
activation energy definition

common_mistakes.feedback: C shows the arrow from the baseline up to the peak — that's the overall energy change ΔH (reactants to products) for the endothermic reaction, not the activation energy. Ea is measured only from the reactants line up to the peak.

D is the arrow from the peak down to the products line — that's the energy released as the activated complex relaxes to products. This drop only represents an activation energy for an exothermic reaction in reverse; for this endothermic profile it is NOT the activation energy.

A is a short arrow showing the energy of the reactants above the x-axis, not the extra energy needed to reach the activated complex. Don't confuse a diagram of an endothermic reaction with an exothermic one — here products are HIGHER than reactants, and Ea is the height of the hump above the reactants line.

parts.model_answer_html:
Answer: B

This is an endothermic energy profile because the products line is higher than the reactants line. The activation energy (Ea) is the minimum energy colliding particles must have to react, shown on the diagram as the height of the hump measured from the reactants line up to the peak.

  • A — energy of the reactants above the baseline (not Ea).
  • B — arrow from the reactants dashed line up to the peak = activation energy. ✓
  • C — arrow from the baseline up to the peak = overall energy change ΔH (reactants → products).
  • D — arrow from the peak down to the products line = energy given out from the activated complex to the products.
chapter_refs: C5.01 activation energy
C5.01 endothermic profile

questions.stem: Which reaction pathway diagram shows the activation energy for an exothermic reaction?

parts.prompt: Which reaction pathway diagram shows the activation energy for an exothermic reaction?

mcq_options.label: A
mcq_options.text: Endothermic profile (products drawn higher than reactants); activation-energy arrow labelled near the top of the hump.

mcq_options.label: B
mcq_options.text: Endothermic profile (products drawn higher than reactants); activation-energy arrow drawn from the reactants line up to the peak.

mcq_options.label: C
mcq_options.text: Exothermic profile (products drawn lower than reactants); activation-energy arrow drawn from the reactants line up to the peak of the hump.

mcq_options.label: D
mcq_options.text: Exothermic profile (products drawn lower than reactants); arrow labelled 'activation energy' drawn from the peak down to the products line.

accepted_phrasings: C

required_working_concepts.concept: Exothermic profile: products lower than reactants (thermal energy released to surroundings, ΔH negative)
Activation energy is the height of the hump from the reactants line up to the peak (minimum energy colliding particles must have to react)

common_mistakes.feedback: A shows an endothermic profile — the products line is drawn HIGHER than the reactants line, so thermal energy is absorbed, not released. For an exothermic reaction the products must be LOWER than the reactants.

B also shows an endothermic profile (products above reactants). The activation-energy arrow in B is drawn correctly (reactants line up to the peak), but the reaction is not exothermic, so B fails the exothermic test.

D is exothermic, but the labelled arrow goes from the peak DOWN to the products line — that is the total thermal energy released (|ΔH|), NOT the activation energy. The activation energy must be drawn from the reactants line UP to the peak of the hump.

parts.model_answer_html:
Answer: C.

Two conditions must both be satisfied:

  1. Exothermic profile: exothermic reactions release thermal energy to the surroundings, so the products line must be drawn lower than the reactants line (ΔH is negative). This eliminates diagrams A and B, where the products are higher than the reactants (endothermic).
  2. Activation energy labelled correctly: activation energy (Ea) is the minimum energy that colliding particles must have to react, and on a reaction pathway diagram it is drawn as the vertical height from the reactants line up to the peak of the hump. In diagram D the arrow is drawn from the peak down to the products line — that is the energy released (|ΔH|), not Ea. In diagram C the arrow runs from the reactants line up to the top of the hump, which is exactly Ea.

Diagram C is the only one that satisfies both conditions, so C is correct.
chapter_refs: C5.01 activation energy
C5.01 exothermic reaction profile

questions.stem: A reaction pathway diagram for a reaction is shown (energy vs progress of reaction with marked energy change X from peak down to products). Which statement describes and explains energy change X?

parts.prompt: Which statement describes AND explains energy change X (the downward arrow from the peak of the reaction pathway diagram to the products line)?

mcq_options.label: A
mcq_options.text: Thermal energy is given out as bonds break.

mcq_options.label: B
mcq_options.text: Thermal energy is given out as bonds form.

mcq_options.label: C
mcq_options.text: Thermal energy is taken in as bonds break.

mcq_options.label: D
mcq_options.text: Thermal energy is taken in as bonds form.

required_working_concepts.concept: X points downward, so the products are at lower energy than the peak and energy is released (given out)
On the descent new product bonds are forming (bond making), which releases thermal energy

common_mistakes.feedback: You got the direction right (energy IS given out on the descent), but you flipped the bond step. Bond BREAKING takes energy IN (endothermic) — that is what happens on the UP part of the curve from reactants to the peak. On the way DOWN (arrow X), the free atoms are FORMING new product bonds, and making bonds releases energy. Remember MEXOBENDO: Making bonds = EXOthermic, Breaking bonds = ENDOthermic. Correct answer: B.

Two things went wrong. (1) X points DOWN, so energy is being given OUT, not taken in — the products sit lower than the peak. (2) On the descent, atoms are FORMING new product bonds (not breaking them); bond breaking already finished at the top of the hump. So both the energy direction and the bond step are the wrong way round. The correct answer is B: thermal energy is given out as bonds form.

You correctly spotted that new bonds are forming on the descent, but you flipped the energy direction. Bond MAKING releases energy to the surroundings (exothermic), so X, which points DOWNWARD from the peak to the products line, shows energy being given OUT, not taken in. Remember MEXOBENDO: Making bonds = EXOthermic. Correct answer: B.

parts.model_answer_html:
Answer: B — thermal energy is given out as bonds form.

1. Read the diagram. X is the arrow pointing DOWN from the peak of the curve to the products line. A downward arrow on an energy-vs-progress diagram means energy is being given out to the surroundings.

2. What is happening at the peak? At the top of the hump the original reactant bonds have been fully broken — the atoms are momentarily free (the transition state). Breaking bonds is what took energy IN on the UP side of the curve (that is the activation energy), not what X represents.

3. What is happening on the descent (X)? The free atoms rearrange and form new product bonds. Bond FORMING is exothermic — it releases thermal energy, which is why the curve drops down to the products line. So X shows thermal energy being given out as new bonds form.

Mnemonic — MEXOBENDO: Making bonds = EXOthermic (energy out), Breaking bonds = ENDOthermic (energy in). X is on the descent, energy goes out, so bonds must be forming. That matches only option B.
chapter_refs: C5.01 reaction pathway diagrams (energy vs progress of reaction)
C5.01 bond making is exothermic (energy given out)
C5.01 bond breaking is endothermic (energy taken in)
MEXOBENDO mnemonic: Making bonds = EXOthermic, Breaking bonds = ENDOthermic

questions.stem: The initial and final temperatures of four different reactions are recorded. Which reaction is the most exothermic?

parts.prompt: Which reaction is the most exothermic? Compare the initial and final temperatures for each option and pick the one with the greatest temperature rise of the surroundings.

mcq_options.label: A
mcq_options.text: initial temperature 19 °C; final temperature 16 °C

mcq_options.label: B
mcq_options.text: initial temperature 20 °C; final temperature 19 °C

mcq_options.label: C
mcq_options.text: initial temperature 22 °C; final temperature 24 °C

mcq_options.label: D
mcq_options.text: initial temperature 24 °C; final temperature 25 °C

common_mistakes.feedback: A has the biggest temperature CHANGE in size (|−3| = 3), but look at the SIGN of ΔT. The temperature FELL from 19 °C to 16 °C (ΔT = −3 °C), so the surroundings got COLDER. When the surroundings cool down, the reaction took energy IN from them — that is ENDOTHERMIC, not exothermic. Remember: exothermic warms the surroundings, so ΔT must be POSITIVE.

Same trap as A — you probably focused on the number of degrees changed and forgot the sign. B goes from 20 °C down to 19 °C (ΔT = −1 °C), so the surroundings COOLED and the reaction is ENDOTHERMIC. Only options where the final temperature is HIGHER than the initial temperature can be exothermic.

Good — you correctly spotted that D is exothermic (temperature went up from 24 °C to 25 °C, ΔT = +1 °C). But the question asks for the MOST exothermic, i.e. the BIGGEST temperature rise. Option C rises by +2 °C (22 °C → 24 °C), which is a larger rise than D’s +1 °C, so C released more energy to the surroundings. C is more exothermic than D.

parts.model_answer_html:
Answer: C

A reaction is exothermic when it transfers thermal energy from the chemicals to the surroundings. The surroundings (the water / solution) therefore warm up, so the measured temperature rises and ΔT = Tfinal − Tinitial is positive. The more exothermic the reaction, the greater the temperature rise.

Calculate ΔT for each row:
A: 16 − 19 = −3 °C → temperature fell → endothermic (reject).
B: 19 − 20 = −1 °C → temperature fell → endothermic (reject).
C: 24 − 22 = +2 °C → temperature rose → exothermic.
D: 25 − 24 = +1 °C → temperature rose → exothermic.

C and D are both exothermic, so compare the size of the rise: C rises by +2 °C, D rises by only +1 °C. C gives the largest temperature increase, so C is the most exothermic reaction.

Watch out: A has the biggest change in magnitude (3 °C), but the temperature drops, so A is the most endothermic, not the most exothermic. Always check the SIGN of ΔT first.
chapter_refs: C5.01 Exothermic reaction: transfers thermal energy to the surroundings; surroundings warm up (ΔT positive).
C5.01 Endothermic reaction: takes thermal energy from the surroundings; surroundings cool down (ΔT negative).
C5.02 Size of the temperature change indicates the amount of energy transferred; a bigger positive ΔT means a more exothermic reaction.

questions.stem: Which reaction is most endothermic? (four options give the initial and final temperatures of the reaction mixture)

parts.prompt: Which reaction is most endothermic?

mcq_options.label: A
mcq_options.text: initial 21 °C, final 19 °C

mcq_options.label: B
mcq_options.text: initial 20 °C, final 22 °C

mcq_options.label: C
mcq_options.text: initial 19 °C, final 18 °C

mcq_options.label: D
mcq_options.text: initial 18 °C, final 23 °C

required_working_concepts.concept: endothermic → surroundings cool
compare temperature drops

common_mistakes.feedback: D has the biggest temperature change in size (+5 °C), but look at the SIGN — the temperature went UP from 18 °C to 23 °C. A rise in temperature means the reaction gave OUT heat, so D is exothermic, not endothermic. Endothermic reactions cool the surroundings (temperature falls).
B also warmed up (20 °C → 22 °C, ΔT = +2 °C), so B is exothermic as well. Remember: endothermic = surroundings lose heat to the reaction, so the thermometer reading DROPS.
C is endothermic — the temperature does drop (19 °C → 18 °C) — but it only falls by 1 °C. A falls by 2 °C, so A takes in MORE heat from the surroundings and is the MOST endothermic.

parts.model_answer_html:
Answer: A

An endothermic reaction takes in thermal energy from the surroundings, so the surroundings (the reaction mixture) cool down and the thermometer reading falls. The MOST endothermic reaction is the one with the biggest temperature drop.

Calculate ΔT = Tfinal − Tinitial for each option:

  • A: 19 − 21 = −2 °C (temperature fell by 2 °C → endothermic) ✓
  • B: 22 − 20 = +2 °C (temperature rose → exothermic).
  • C: 18 − 19 = −1 °C (temperature fell by only 1 °C → endothermic but smaller).
  • D: 23 − 18 = +5 °C (temperature rose → exothermic, even though the change is largest in size).

Only A and C are endothermic, and A has the biggest drop (2 °C vs 1 °C), so A is the most endothermic.
chapter_refs: C5.01 endothermic → surroundings cool
C5.01 temperature change sign
C5.01 comparing endothermic reactions

parts.prompt: Which reaction is the most exothermic? Compare the initial and final temperatures for each option and pick the one with the greatest temperature rise of the surroundings.

mcq_options.label: A
mcq_options.text: initial 19 °C, final 16 °C

mcq_options.label: B
mcq_options.text: initial 20 °C, final 19 °C

mcq_options.label: C
mcq_options.text: initial 22 °C, final 24 °C

mcq_options.label: D
mcq_options.text: initial 24 °C, final 25 °C

common_mistakes.feedback: A has the biggest temperature CHANGE in size (|−3| = 3), but look at the SIGN of ΔT. The temperature FELL from 19 °C to 16 °C (ΔT = −3 °C), so the surroundings got COLDER. When the surroundings cool down, the reaction took energy IN from them — that is ENDOTHERMIC, not exothermic. Remember: exothermic warms the surroundings, so ΔT must be POSITIVE.

Good — you correctly spotted that D is exothermic (temperature went up from 24 °C to 25 °C, ΔT = +1 °C). But the question asks for the MOST exothermic, i.e. the BIGGEST temperature rise. Option C rises by +2 °C (22 °C → 24 °C), which is a larger rise than D’s +1 °C, so C released more energy to the surroundings. C is more exothermic than D.

Answer: C

A reaction is exothermic when it transfers thermal energy from the chemicals to the surroundings. The surroundings (the water / solution) therefore warm up, so the measured temperature rises and ΔT = Tfinal − Tinitial is positive. The more exothermic the reaction, the greater the temperature rise.

Calculate ΔT for each row:
A: 16 − 19 = −3 °C → temperature fell → endothermic (reject).
B: 19 − 20 = −1 °C → temperature fell → endothermic (reject).

D: 25 − 24 = +1 °C → temperature rose → exothermic.

questions.stem: The reaction pathway diagrams for reaction X and for reaction Y are shown. Which statement about the reactions is correct? (Reaction X: reactants are higher than products. Reaction Y: products are higher than reactants.)

parts.prompt: Which statement about the reactions is correct?

mcq_options.label: A
mcq_options.text: Reaction X has a greater activation energy than reaction Y.

mcq_options.label: B
mcq_options.text: Reaction X is endothermic and reaction Y is exothermic.

mcq_options.label: C
mcq_options.text: The overall energy change in reaction X is much greater than in reaction Y.

mcq_options.label: D
mcq_options.text: The temperature increases during reaction X and decreases during reaction Y.

required_working_concepts.concept: Read exothermic vs endothermic from pathway heights
Link exothermic/endothermic to surroundings temperature

common_mistakes.feedback: You picked B, but that has it flipped. Reaction X shows reactants HIGHER than products, so X releases energy — it is EXOTHERMIC. Reaction Y shows products HIGHER than reactants, so Y takes in energy — it is ENDOTHERMIC. B says the opposite.

You picked A. Activation energy is the height of the HUMP measured above the reactants, not the vertical gap between reactants and products. In this diagram the two humps look about the same size, so you cannot claim X has a greater activation energy than Y.

You picked C. 'Much greater' is a quantitative claim, but the diagrams do not give any numerical energy values, and the overall energy gaps for X and Y look similar. Without numbers you cannot say one is much greater than the other.

parts.model_answer_html:
Answer: D

  1. In reaction X the reactants are higher than the products, so energy is released to the surroundings — X is exothermic, and the surroundings warm up.
  2. In reaction Y the products are higher than the reactants, so energy is absorbed from the surroundings — Y is endothermic, and the surroundings cool down.
  3. So the temperature increases during X and decreases during Y — this is statement D.

Why not the others? A: activation energy is the hump above the reactants, not the reactant–product gap, and the humps look similar here. B: has X and Y swapped. C: 'much greater' needs numbers, and the diagrams do not give any.
chapter_refs: exothermic_endothermic_from_pathway_diagram
surroundings_temperature_change
activation_energy_vs_overall_energy_change

questions.stem: The reaction between aluminium and iron(III) oxide is exothermic. A flame is used to provide the activation energy to start the reaction. Fig. 5.1 shows the incomplete reaction pathway diagram for this reaction. Complete Fig. 5.1. Include: the energy level of the products; a labelled arrow to show the activation energy, Ea; a labelled arrow to show the overall energy change of the reaction. [3]

parts.prompt: Complete Fig. 5.1 by adding the products energy level, a labelled activation-energy arrow (Ea), and a labelled arrow for the overall energy change of the reaction.
accepted_phrasings: products line drawn below reactants line
hump / peak drawn above reactants line
Ea arrow from reactants line up to the peak
overall energy change arrow from reactants line down to products line
energy released / exothermic / ΔH negative

required_working_concepts.concept: Products line drawn below reactants line (exothermic profile)
Activation energy labelled as height from reactants line to peak (Ea)
hints: reactants line up to top of hump

required_working_concepts.concept: Overall enthalpy change shown as downward arrow from reactants to products labelled energy released / ΔH

common_mistakes.feedback: The products line should be drawn BELOW the reactants line, not above – this reaction is exothermic, so energy decreases from reactants to products.

Ea is the height from the reactants line up to the peak of the hump, not the gap between reactants and products. The reactants-to-products gap is the overall energy change (ΔH), not the activation energy.
You must label the activation-energy arrow (e.g. 'Ea' or 'activation energy'). An unlabelled arrow does not score the activation-energy mark.
Label the downward arrow from reactants to products with the overall energy change (accept 'ΔH', 'energy released', 'enthalpy change of reaction'). Without the label you lose the third mark.

parts.model_answer_html:
On Fig. 5.1 you must add three things to score all 3 marks:

  1. Products energy level (1 mark). Draw a horizontal line labelled products clearly below the reactants line, and join the two lines with a smooth hump whose peak sits above the reactants line. The reaction is exothermic, so products must be lower than reactants.
  2. Activation energy, Ea (1 mark). Draw a vertical arrow from the reactants line up to the top of the hump and label it Ea (or 'activation energy'). This is the energy the flame supplies to start the reaction.
  3. Overall energy change of the reaction (1 mark). Draw a vertical arrow from the reactants line down to the products line and label it overall energy change (accept 'ΔH', 'energy released', 'enthalpy change'). Because the reaction is exothermic, ΔH is negative and energy is released to the surroundings.

Common traps: drawing products above reactants (that would be endothermic); labelling the reactants-to-products gap as Ea (that gap is ΔH, not Ea); leaving arrows unlabelled.
chapter_refs: C5.01 reaction pathway diagram (exothermic profile)
C5.01 activation energy Ea
C5.01 overall energy change ΔH (exothermic = negative)

questions.stem: The reaction pathway diagram for a reaction is shown (energy axis with levels X at reactants, Y above reactants at the products line, Z at the peak). Which statement about the reaction is correct?

parts.prompt: Select the correct statement about the reaction shown in the pathway diagram.

mcq_options.label: A
mcq_options.text: The activation energy for this reaction is equal to the value of (Z − Y).

mcq_options.label: B
mcq_options.text: The energy released by this reaction is equal to the value of (Y − X).

mcq_options.label: C
mcq_options.text: The energy used to break bonds is more than the energy released in forming bonds.

mcq_options.label: D
mcq_options.text: The overall energy change for this reaction is equal to the value of (Z − X).

required_working_concepts.concept: Identify reaction as endothermic from pathway diagram (products above reactants).
Endothermic bond-energy rule: energy to break bonds > energy released making bonds.

common_mistakes.feedback: Option A uses the wrong formula. Activation energy is the peak height above the REACTANTS line, so Ea = Z − X, not Z − Y. The reactants sit at X, not Y.
Option B is wrong because the reaction is endothermic — the products line is above the reactants line, so energy is ABSORBED, not released. (Y − X) is the energy taken IN, not given out.
Option D confuses two different things. (Z − X) is the activation energy (peak minus reactants), not the overall energy change. The overall energy change is products minus reactants, i.e. (Y − X).

parts.model_answer_html:
Answer: C

The diagram shows reactants at level X, a peak at Z, and the products line at level Y, with Y above X. Because products are higher than reactants, the reaction is endothermic.

  • A is wrong: activation energy = peak − reactants = (Z − X), not (Z − Y).
  • B is wrong: the reaction is endothermic, so energy is absorbed, not released. (Y − X) is the energy taken in.
  • D is wrong: (Z − X) is the activation energy, not the overall energy change. The overall energy change is (Y − X).
  • C is correct: in an endothermic reaction the energy needed to break bonds is greater than the energy released when new bonds form — this matches the diagram.
chapter_refs: Slide 4: reaction pathway diagrams
Slide 5: endothermic bond-energy rule
Slide 6: activation energy definition

questions.stem: Reaction of copper with oxygen. Fig. 4.2 is a reaction pathway diagram for a reaction catalysed by copper. On the diagram, the reactants are on a LOWER energy level than the products and there is an activation-energy hump between them. Answer parts (c)(i)–(c)(iv).

parts.prompt: Identify the type of chemical reaction shown in Fig. 4.2 and explain your answer. [2]
accepted_phrasings: endothermic because products are higher in energy than reactants
endothermic; energy is absorbed / taken in from the surroundings
endothermic — the product energy level is above the reactant energy level

required_working_concepts.concept: Correct reaction type
Justification from the diagram

common_mistakes.feedback: You wrote 'exothermic'. Look at Fig. 4.2 again: the products are drawn HIGHER than the reactants. When products are higher than reactants, energy is ABSORBED, so the reaction is ENDOTHERMIC. (Even though in real life copper + oxygen is exothermic, you must answer from the diagram as drawn.)
Your explanation needs to compare the two energy levels. Say that the products are at a HIGHER energy level than the reactants on the diagram (or that energy is absorbed from the surroundings).

parts.model_answer_html: Endothermic [1]. The products are at a higher energy level than the reactants on Fig. 4.2, so energy is absorbed (taken in) from the surroundings [1].
chapter_refs: Chemical Energetics — endothermic vs exothermic from a reaction pathway diagram

parts.prompt: Draw an arrow on Fig. 4.2 to show the activation energy for this reaction. [1]
accepted_phrasings: arrow from reactants energy level up to peak
vertical arrow from the reactants line to the top of the hump

required_working_concepts.concept: Arrow starts at reactants' energy level and ends at the top of the activation-energy peak
hints: bottom of left line to top of hump

common_mistakes.feedback: The activation energy arrow does NOT start at the product line. It starts at the REACTANTS' energy level and goes up to the top of the hump. An arrow from reactants to products would be ΔH, not Ea.
ΔH (enthalpy change) is the reactant-to-product energy gap. Activation energy is just the reactants-to-peak height. Draw the arrow only up to the top of the hump.

parts.model_answer_html: A single vertical arrow that starts on the reactants' energy line (the flat line on the left) and ends at the very top of the hump is correct [1]. Label it Ea if space allows.
chapter_refs: Chemical Energetics — representing activation energy on a reaction pathway diagram

parts.prompt: Define activation energy. [2]
accepted_phrasings: the minimum energy colliding particles must have for a reaction to occur
the minimum energy needed to start a reaction
the minimum energy reactant particles need to react

required_working_concepts.concept: 'Minimum' (or equivalent: 'least') amount of energy
For a reaction to occur / for particles to react on collision

common_mistakes.feedback: You need the word 'minimum' (or 'least'). Activation energy is the MINIMUM energy required — not just 'the energy needed'.
Activation energy is about the energy of PARTICLES (usually kinetic energy), not heat specifically. Say 'minimum energy particles must have to react', not 'the heat needed'.

parts.model_answer_html: The minimum amount of energy [1] that colliding reactant particles must have in order for a reaction to occur (to start bonds breaking) [1].
chapter_refs: Chemical Energetics — definition of activation energy

parts.prompt: Describe what is meant by a catalyst. [2]
accepted_phrasings: a substance that increases the rate of a reaction and is not used up
speeds up a reaction; chemically unchanged at the end
increases the rate of reaction; can be recovered unchanged

required_working_concepts.concept: Increases the rate of reaction / speeds up the reaction
Not used up / chemically unchanged at the end

common_mistakes.feedback: You only scored the 'speeds up' mark. A catalyst also has to be NOT USED UP (chemically unchanged / recoverable) at the end of the reaction. Add this second idea for the second mark.
'Helps the reaction' is too vague. Say it INCREASES THE RATE (or speeds up) the reaction.
A catalyst does NOT change the reaction or its products — it only changes how fast it happens. Say 'speeds up the reaction without being used up'.

parts.model_answer_html:
A catalyst is a substance that increases the rate of a chemical reaction [1] and is not used up (is chemically unchanged) at the end of the reaction [1].

Optional extension: it does this by providing an alternative pathway with a lower activation energy.
chapter_refs: Chemical Energetics — catalysts lower the activation-energy hump on a reaction pathway diagram and are not used up

questions.stem: Question 4 (continued). The reaction between aqueous sodium hydroxide and dilute hydrochloric acid transfers thermal energy to the surroundings.

parts.prompt: Tick (✓) all the statements that are true for aqueous sodium hydroxide: (1) It is a covalent substance. (2) It turns universal indicator purple. (3) It reacts with dilute sulfuric acid to form sodium sulfate. (4) It is a base but not an alkali.

required_working_concepts.concept: Tick statement 2 — aqueous NaOH is a strong alkali (pH ~13–14) so universal indicator turns purple/dark blue.
Tick statement 3 — NaOH neutralises dilute sulfuric acid: 2NaOH + H2SO4 → Na2SO4 + 2H2O.

common_mistakes.feedback: NaOH is IONIC (Na+ and OH- ions), not covalent — do not tick statement 1.
NaOH IS an alkali. An alkali is a base that dissolves in water, and NaOH is very soluble — do not tick statement 4.

parts.model_answer_html:
Tick statement 2 and statement 3 only.

  • Statement 1 — FALSE. NaOH is ionic (Na⁺ OH⁻), not covalent.
  • Statement 2 — TRUE. Aqueous NaOH is a strong alkali (pH ≈ 13–14), so universal indicator turns dark blue / purple.
  • Statement 3 — TRUE. 2NaOH + H₂SO₄ → Na₂SO₄ + 2H₂O (acid + alkali → salt + water).
  • Statement 4 — FALSE. NaOH is a soluble base, and a soluble base is an alkali.
chapter_refs: C5.01 properties of alkalis

parts.prompt: Give one conclusion about the energy involved in bond breaking and bond making in this reaction.
accepted_phrasings: more energy is released in bond making than is absorbed in bond breaking
more energy is released when bonds are made than is absorbed when bonds are broken
bond making releases more energy than bond breaking absorbs
the energy given out when bonds form is greater than the energy taken in to break bonds
energy released from forming bonds is greater than energy needed to break bonds
bond forming releases more energy than bond breaking takes in
exothermic because bond making releases more energy than bond breaking absorbs

common_mistakes.feedback: Breaking bonds ABSORBS energy (endothermic); only MAKING bonds releases energy. Remember MEXOBENDO — Making=EXO, Breaking=ENDO.
You must compare the two quantities — say which process releases or absorbs MORE energy (bond making releases more than bond breaking absorbs).
Bond breaking does NOT release energy — it absorbs it. You've got MEXOBENDO the wrong way round.

parts.model_answer_html:
More energy is released when bonds are made (in the products) than is absorbed when bonds are broken (in the reactants). That is why the reaction transfers thermal energy to the surroundings — it is exothermic.

Slide 6 — MEXOBENDO: Making bonds = EXOthermic, Breaking bonds = ENDOthermic.
chapter_refs: MEXOBENDO
C5.01 bond making exothermic

parts.prompt: Complete Fig. 4.2 to show the reaction pathway diagram for this reaction. Label the reactants and products, and show the overall energy change using an arrow.

required_working_concepts.concept: Reactants line drawn HIGHER than the products line (exothermic profile — products are lower in energy than reactants).
Both levels correctly labelled: upper line = reactants (NaOH + HCl); lower line = products (NaCl + H2O).
A vertical arrow drawn between the two levels pointing DOWN from reactants to products, labelled 'overall energy change' (or ΔH, or 'energy given out' / 'energy released').

common_mistakes.feedback: This reaction is exothermic, so products are BELOW reactants, not above. Products-above is the endothermic profile — wrong direction for MEXOBENDO here.
You must draw an arrow showing the overall energy change and label it (ΔH or 'energy given out'). Missing the arrow loses 1 mark.
For an exothermic reaction the overall-energy-change arrow points DOWN (from reactants down to products), because energy is released to the surroundings.
Every line and arrow needs a label — reactants, products, and overall energy change. Unlabelled diagrams don't score.

parts.model_answer_html:
Because the reaction transfers thermal energy to the surroundings, it is exothermic, so the products line must end below the reactants line.

  1. Draw a short horizontal line at the top-left of the axes — label it reactants: NaOH + HCl.
  2. Draw a short horizontal line at a LOWER energy on the right — label it products: NaCl + H₂O.
  3. Between the two levels draw a vertical arrow pointing downwards from the reactants level to the products level, and label it overall energy change (or ΔH, or 'energy given out').

The arrow points down because energy leaves the system — MEXOBENDO (slide 6): Making bonds gives out more energy than Breaking bonds absorbs, so the net change is negative.
chapter_refs: C5.01 exothermic profile
MEXOBENDO

questions.stem: Fig. 6.1 shows the combustion of magnesium ribbon in air held by tongs; a white powder of magnesium oxide is formed.

parts.prompt: Name the gas that reacts with the magnesium.
accepted_phrasings: oxygen
O2
O₂
oxygen gas
dioxygen

common_mistakes.feedback: Air is mostly nitrogen, but nitrogen is very unreactive at this temperature. Magnesium burns by reacting with oxygen (about 21% of air) to form magnesium oxide (MgO).
Carbon dioxide does not form MgO. The product is a metal-oxygen compound, so the reactant gas must contain only oxygen — it is O2.
'Air' is a mixture. The question asks for the specific gas in air that reacts — name oxygen.

parts.model_answer_html:
oxygen (O2).

Magnesium is a metal and it combines with oxygen from the air to form magnesium oxide: 2Mg + O2 → 2MgO. In Worked Example C5.01 this same combustion is used as the exothermic reaction for the energy-level diagram.
chapter_refs: C5.01 Worked Example: Mg + O2 combustion

parts.prompt: Magnesium oxide is a metallic oxide. Circle the term that describes this type of oxide: acidic / aqueous / basic / carbonate / solvent.

mcq_options.label: A
mcq_options.text: acidic

mcq_options.label: B
mcq_options.text: aqueous

mcq_options.label: C
mcq_options.text: basic

mcq_options.label: D
mcq_options.text: carbonate

mcq_options.label: E
mcq_options.text: solvent

accepted_phrasings: basic

common_mistakes.feedback: That's reversed — acidic oxides are non-metal oxides such as CO2 or SO2. Metal oxides like MgO are BASIC (they react with acids to make a salt + water).
'Aqueous' means dissolved in water — it describes a solution, not a class of oxide. The correct category is 'basic'.
A carbonate must contain the CO3^2- ion (e.g. CaCO3). MgO has no carbon — it is a basic oxide.

parts.model_answer_html:
basic.

Magnesium is a metal, and metal oxides are basic — they neutralise acids (e.g. MgO + 2HCl → MgCl2 + H2O). Non-metal oxides like CO2 and SO2 are acidic.

Note: this sub-part is acid-base chemistry, not chemical energetics — it is outside the C5 chapter.
chapter_refs: out-of-scope for C5 Chemical Energetics (acid/base oxide classification)

parts.prompt: This reaction is exothermic. Describe what is meant by exothermic.
accepted_phrasings: energy/heat is released to the surroundings
heat is given out to the surroundings
thermal energy is transferred to the surroundings so the surroundings get hotter
a reaction that gives out heat (and causes the temperature of the surroundings to rise)

required_working_concepts.concept: reaction transfers (thermal) energy / heat OUT to the surroundings (surroundings warm up)

common_mistakes.feedback: That's the opposite! Endothermic = energy taken IN from the surroundings (surroundings get colder). Exothermic = energy given OUT to the surroundings (surroundings get hotter). Remember: 'exo' = out.
Careful — absorbing/taking-in energy describes ENDOthermic reactions. An EXOthermic reaction RELEASES energy to the surroundings.
Energy 'taken in' from the surroundings is endothermic. Exothermic is energy 'given out' to the surroundings.
Mention WHERE the energy goes. For full marks say the heat/energy is released to the SURROUNDINGS (so the surroundings get hotter).

parts.model_answer_html:
Exothermic means the reaction releases (thermal) energy — heat — to the surroundings, so the surroundings get hotter (the temperature of the surroundings rises).

In this experiment you can see and feel the energy leaving the system: the burning magnesium gives out a bright white light and heat. On an energy-level diagram the products (MgO) sit below the reactants (Mg + O2); the difference is the energy released to the surroundings.
chapter_refs: C5.01 Worked Example: 2Mg + O2 -> 2MgO releases heat and light (exothermic)
C5 definition: exothermic = energy given out to surroundings; surroundings warm up

questions.stem: Which statement indicates that a chemical change occurs?

parts.prompt: Which statement indicates that a chemical change occurs?

mcq_options.label: A
mcq_options.text: Energy is absorbed.

mcq_options.label: B
mcq_options.text: There is a change in colour.

mcq_options.label: C
mcq_options.text: There is a change of state.

mcq_options.label: D
mcq_options.text: There is no change in mass.

common_mistakes.feedback: You probably remembered that endothermic reactions absorb energy, so 'energy absorbed' felt like a chemical-change clue. But chapter slide 11 (point 2) reminds us that both physical and chemical changes involve energy changes - melting ice absorbs energy without making any new substance. So 'energy is absorbed' alone does not prove a chemical change.

A change of state (solid ↔ liquid ↔ gas) is a physical change - the substance itself is unchanged, only the arrangement of its particles. No new chemical has formed, so this cannot be the indicator of a chemical change.

Mass is conserved in essentially every closed-system reaction, chemical or physical, so 'no change in mass' cannot tell the two apart. The question asks for an indicator that something new has formed, not for a conservation law.

parts.model_answer_html:
B. There is a change in colour.
A new colour means a new substance has been formed - for example copper turning black as it reacts with oxygen to form copper(II) oxide. Forming a new substance is the defining feature of a chemical change.
The other options do not uniquely indicate a chemical change: A (energy absorbed) and D (no change in mass) happen in physical changes too, and C (change of state) is itself a physical change.
chapter_refs: C5.01 Exothermic and endothermic reactions
C5.01 physical-and-chemical changes both involve energy changes

questions.stem: Which statement explains why the rate of a reaction is greater at a higher temperature?

parts.prompt: Which statement explains why the rate of a reaction is greater at a higher temperature?

mcq_options.label: A
mcq_options.text: The activation energy is higher.

mcq_options.label: B
mcq_options.text: More colliding particles have the minimum energy to react.

mcq_options.label: C
mcq_options.text: The frequency of collisions between reacting particles decreases.

mcq_options.label: D
mcq_options.text: There are more reacting particles per unit volume.

common_mistakes.feedback: Careful - activation energy (Ea) is a fixed property of the reaction, set by the bonds that must be broken. Temperature does NOT raise Ea; it only changes how many particles have enough energy to reach it. From your chapter: 'Activation energy is the minimum energy required for colliding particles to react successfully.'

This is backwards. Higher temperature → faster particles → more collisions per second, not fewer. Rate goes up for two linked reasons: collisions are more frequent, AND a greater fraction of collisions now clear the activation-energy barrier (which is the primary reason the exam wants).

Changing temperature does not change how many particles are in the container - that would describe a change in concentration, not temperature. Raising T raises the energy of the particles you already have, not their number.

parts.model_answer_html:
B. More colliding particles have the minimum energy to react.
Activation energy (Ea) is the minimum energy colliding particles need in order to react successfully. When the temperature rises, the average kinetic energy of the particles rises, so a greater fraction of collisions now have energy ≥ Ea and succeed - which is why the reaction rate increases.
A is wrong because Ea is a property of the reaction and is not changed by temperature. C is the wrong direction (collisions get MORE frequent). D describes concentration, not temperature.
chapter_refs: C5.01 activation energy
C5.01 KEY WORDS activation energy definition

questions.stem: Calcium oxide, CaO, is used to make cement. Calcium oxide is formed by the decomposition of calcium carbonate, CaCO3, at a very high temperature. The equation is: CaCO3(s) → CaO(s) + CO2(g). Answer parts (a)(i) and (a)(ii).

parts.prompt: Suggest why making cement contributes to global warming. [2]
accepted_phrasings: decomposing calcium carbonate releases carbon dioxide; CO2 is a greenhouse gas so more of it causes global warming
the reaction produces CO2; CO2 traps heat / is a greenhouse gas / enhances the greenhouse effect
burning fossil fuels to reach the high temperature releases CO2 which is a greenhouse gas, causing global warming

required_working_concepts.concept: Identifies that CO2 is released (either from the decomposition itself or from the fossil fuels burnt to reach the high temperature)
Links CO2 to the greenhouse effect / global warming (CO2 is a greenhouse gas that traps heat)

common_mistakes.feedback: You did not mention carbon dioxide. The equation tells you that decomposing CaCO3 produces CO2; also, the very high temperature is normally reached by burning fossil fuels, which releases more CO2. Name CO2 explicitly to score the first mark.

You named the gas but did not explain WHY CO2 causes global warming. Say that CO2 is a greenhouse gas — it absorbs thermal (infrared) radiation and traps heat in the atmosphere — to score the second mark.
Global warming is NOT caused by damage to the ozone layer — that is a different environmental issue. The link here is CO2 → greenhouse gas → global warming.

parts.model_answer_html:
Two mark points, any one from each row:

  1. Source of CO2 [1]: the thermal decomposition of CaCO3 releases CO2 directly (from the equation), and/or the very high temperature is reached by burning fossil fuels, which also releases CO2.
  2. Greenhouse link [1]: CO2 is a greenhouse gas — it absorbs infrared radiation re-emitted by the Earth and traps heat in the atmosphere, which causes global warming.

Cross-chapter note: the greenhouse effect is covered in more detail in C13 Atmosphere; here we only need to know CO2 is a greenhouse gas.
chapter_refs: C5.01 endothermic thermal decomposition of CaCO3 produces CO2
C5.01 endothermic reactions need a continuous supply of thermal energy
Cross-chapter: C13 Atmosphere — CO2 as a greenhouse gas

parts.prompt: The reaction is endothermic. Explain what is meant by an endothermic reaction. Use ideas about making bonds and breaking bonds in your answer. [3]
accepted_phrasings: an endothermic reaction takes in thermal energy from the surroundings; breaking bonds absorbs energy; making bonds releases energy; energy absorbed when breaking bonds is greater than energy released when making bonds
endothermic = absorbs energy from surroundings; bond breaking is endothermic, bond making is exothermic; overall bond-breaking energy > bond-making energy
takes in heat; more energy is needed to break the bonds in the reactants than is given out when new bonds are formed in the products

required_working_concepts.concept: Endothermic = takes in / absorbs thermal energy from the surroundings (ΔH positive; surroundings get colder)
Bond breaking is endothermic (absorbs energy) AND bond making is exothermic (releases energy) — MEXOBENDO
For endothermic reactions the energy absorbed to break the reactant bonds is GREATER than the energy released when new product bonds are made (net energy is taken in)
hints: more energy to break than released by making

common_mistakes.feedback: The reaction is endothermic, not exothermic. In an exothermic reaction energy is GIVEN OUT to the surroundings (ΔH negative). Flip the direction: endothermic = energy TAKEN IN from the surroundings.

The question explicitly asks you to use ideas about bond BREAKING and bond MAKING. You need to say that breaking bonds ABSORBS energy and making bonds RELEASES energy, and that for an endothermic reaction the breaking total is GREATER than the making total.
You have MEXOBENDO inverted. Making bonds RELEASES energy (exothermic); breaking bonds ABSORBS energy (endothermic). Try again with the correct direction.

parts.model_answer_html:
Three mark points:

  1. Energy direction [1]: an endothermic reaction TAKES IN (absorbs) thermal energy from the surroundings, so the surroundings get colder and ΔH is positive.
  2. Bond energies — MEXOBENDO [1]: Breaking the bonds in the reactants absorbs energy (endothermic); making the new bonds in the products releases energy (exothermic).
  3. Net balance [1]: in an endothermic reaction the energy needed to break the reactant bonds is GREATER than the energy released when the new product bonds form, so net energy has to be supplied from the surroundings.

For CaCO3 → CaO + CO2 that net intake is why a very high temperature has to be maintained for the reaction to continue.
chapter_refs: C5.01 endothermic reaction definition (takes in thermal energy, ΔH positive)
C5.01 MEXOBENDO — bond breaking endothermic, bond making exothermic
C5.01 overall ΔH = energy absorbed (breaking) − energy released (making); endothermic when breaking > making

questions.stem: Continuation of Question 5 about the endothermic decomposition of CaCO3 and related calcium compounds. (a)(iii) uses Fig. 5.1 — the incomplete reaction pathway diagram for the endothermic reaction; only the reactants line and the start of the activation-energy hump are drawn. (b) is about calcium hydroxide, Ca(OH)2, as an alkali. Answer parts (a)(iii), (b)(i) and (b)(ii).

parts.prompt: Fig. 5.1 shows the incomplete reaction pathway diagram for the endothermic reaction CaCO3 → CaO + CO2. Complete Fig. 5.1 by including: (1) the energy level of the products, (2) a labelled arrow showing the activation energy Ea, and (3) a labelled arrow showing the overall energy change of the reaction. [3]
accepted_phrasings: products line drawn above reactants; Ea arrow from reactants line to peak; overall energy change arrow from reactants line up to products line
products above reactants (endothermic); Ea labelled from reactants to peak of hump; ΔH labelled upward from reactants to products
products higher; activation energy = reactants to peak; overall energy change = reactants to products (upward, positive)

required_working_concepts.concept: Products energy level drawn ABOVE the reactants line (endothermic profile)
Activation energy Ea labelled with an arrow from the REACTANTS line UP to the PEAK of the hump
hints: reactants line to top of hump

required_working_concepts.concept: Overall energy change (ΔH) labelled with an UPWARD arrow from the REACTANTS line to the PRODUCTS line (endothermic ⇒ positive)

common_mistakes.feedback: You drew the products LOWER than the reactants — that is the EXOTHERMIC profile. This reaction is endothermic (takes in heat), so the products line must sit ABOVE the reactants line.

The activation-energy arrow does NOT go from the peak down to the products line — that is the energy released as the activated complex falls to the product level. Ea is the arrow from the REACTANTS line UP to the peak of the hump.
A downward ΔH arrow shows energy RELEASED (exothermic). This reaction is endothermic, so the overall energy change arrow must point UP from the reactants line to the (higher) products line.
Remember to LABEL the activation-energy arrow as Ea (or 'activation energy'). Unlabelled arrows do not score the Ea mark.

parts.model_answer_html:
Three mark points — each arrow/line has to be drawn AND labelled on Fig. 5.1:

  1. Products line [1]: a horizontal line labelled products drawn ABOVE the reactants line (because the reaction is endothermic). The peak of the existing hump must still sit above this new products line.
  2. Activation energy arrow [1]: an upward arrow that STARTS on the reactants line and ENDS at the very top of the hump, labelled Ea (or activation energy).
  3. Overall energy change arrow [1]: an upward arrow that STARTS on the reactants line and ENDS on the products line, labelled overall energy change (accept ΔH, enthalpy change, energy taken in). Because the reaction is endothermic the arrow points UP and ΔH is positive.
          ╱╲   <— peak (activated complex)
         ╱  ╲
   Ea ↑ ╱    ╲
       ╱      ────── products  ↑
   ───╯                         │ overall energy change (ΔH > 0)
   reactants                    ↓

Common pitfall: drawing the ΔH arrow from the peak down to the products (that is just the drop of the activated complex), or starting Ea from the products line. Ea is always measured from the reactants line up to the peak.
chapter_refs: C5.01 endothermic reaction pathway diagram
C5.01 activation energy Ea — height from reactants line to the peak
C5.01 overall energy change ΔH — reactants line to products line; endothermic ⇒ upward, ΔH positive

parts.prompt: The ions in calcium hydroxide are Ca²⁺ and OH⁻. Deduce the formula for calcium hydroxide. [1]
accepted_phrasings: Ca(OH)2
Ca(OH)₂

required_working_concepts.concept: OUT OF C5 SCOPE — this part tests Acids & Bases / Bonding (C8 / C2). Refer to that chapter when you encounter it. For a quick walk-through: balance the ionic charges (one Ca²⁺ needs two OH⁻) and keep the OH inside brackets because it is a single ion ⇒ Ca(OH)2.
hints: balance charges 2 plus equals 2 times 1 minus

common_mistakes.feedback: CaOH has total charge +1 (not neutral). You need TWO OH⁻ ions to balance the 2+ charge on Ca²⁺, and the OH must stay bracketed because it is a single ion — so Ca(OH)2, not CaOH.
The '2' only applies if the OH stays inside brackets. Write Ca(OH)2 — without the brackets, CaOH2 implies only the H is doubled.

parts.model_answer_html:
Answer: Ca(OH)2 [1].

Ca²⁺ has a +2 charge; OH⁻ has a −1 charge. To get a neutral compound you need two OH⁻ ions per one Ca²⁺, so the formula is Ca(OH)2. The OH stays inside brackets because it is one ion.

⚠️ OUT OF C5 SCOPE: this part tests Acids & Bases / Bonding (C8 / C2). Revisit that chapter when you cover it — you will see the same ionic crossing-over rule applied to lots of salts.
chapter_refs: OUT OF C5 SCOPE — C8 Acids, Bases & Salts (also C2 Bonding)

parts.prompt: Aqueous calcium hydroxide is an alkali. State the colour of methyl orange indicator in aqueous calcium hydroxide. [1]
accepted_phrasings: yellow

required_working_concepts.concept: OUT OF C5 SCOPE — this part tests Acids & Bases (C8). Refer to that chapter when you encounter it. Quick recap: methyl orange is red in acid, yellow in alkali; aqueous Ca(OH)₂ is an alkali (pH ~12) ⇒ the indicator is yellow.

common_mistakes.feedback: Methyl orange is RED in acid, not in alkali. Aqueous Ca(OH)₂ is an alkali, so methyl orange is YELLOW.
Orange is the midpoint colour of methyl orange (neutral-ish). In a clear alkali like aqueous Ca(OH)₂ it goes fully YELLOW.
Purple is the alkali colour of litmus / phenolphthalein (pink), not methyl orange. Methyl orange is YELLOW in an alkali.
Pink is phenolphthalein's alkali colour, not methyl orange's. Methyl orange is YELLOW in an alkali.

parts.model_answer_html:
Answer: yellow [1].

Methyl orange is red in acidic solutions and yellow in alkaline solutions (orange at the midpoint). Aqueous calcium hydroxide is an alkali (pH ~12), so methyl orange turns yellow.

⚠️ OUT OF C5 SCOPE: indicator colours are covered in Acids, Bases & Salts (C8). Refer to that chapter for the full indicator table (litmus, methyl orange, thymolphthalein, phenolphthalein, universal indicator).
chapter_refs: OUT OF C5 SCOPE — C8 Acids, Bases & Salts (indicator colours)

questions.stem: Which arrow on the reaction pathway diagram represents the overall energy change for the reaction?

parts.prompt: Which arrow on the reaction pathway diagram represents the overall energy change for the reaction?

mcq_options.label: A
mcq_options.text: Arrow A (reactants line up to the peak)

mcq_options.label: B
mcq_options.text: Arrow B (small section near peak)

mcq_options.label: C
mcq_options.text: Arrow C (peak down to products line)

mcq_options.label: D
mcq_options.text: Arrow D (reactants line straight to products line)

common_mistakes.feedback: Arrow A goes from reactants up to the peak — that is the activation energy (Ea), not the overall energy change. The overall energy change is the gap between reactants and products lines.

Arrow C goes from the peak down to the products line — that shows the energy released as new bonds form (after Ea has been overcome), not the overall energy change. Look for the arrow that connects reactants directly to products.

parts.model_answer_html: The overall energy change for a reaction is the vertical distance between the reactants line and the products line on a reaction pathway diagram. It is not the activation energy (reactants up to peak) and it is not the energy released going from the peak down to the products line. Arrow D goes straight from the reactants line to the products line, so it represents the overall energy change.
chapter_refs: C5.01 enthalpy change
C5.01 reaction pathway diagram
C5.01 activation energy
